r.message: (Offline Mode) Try describing a specific task like 'finding the shortest path' or 'calculating the sum' to see pre-built algorithmic strategies!

possible.name: Recursion
possible.desc: The simplest approach that explores every possible combination by branching out. While easy to write, it becomes extremely slow as the number of elements grows due to redundant calculations.

possible.name: 0/1 Knapsack
possible.desc: Uses a table to store the results of smaller sub-problems. By building up the solution bottom-up, it avoids re-calculating the same sums, making it vastly more efficient than pure recursion.

possible.name: Meet-in-Middle
possible.desc: A clever optimization for large N. It splits the set into two halves, calculates all possible sums for each, and then combines them using binary search to find the target sum quickly.

possible.name: Greedy
possible.desc: A fast strategy that always picks the largest available element next. It's lightning fast but 'short-sighted'—it doesn't always find the perfect exact sum, making it risky for some problems.

r.reason: Dynamic Programming (Knapsack) is the standard for these problems because it handles constraints reliably and avoids the exponential explosion of recursion.

possible.name: BFS
possible.desc: Ideal for finding the absolute shortest path in unweighted graphs. It explores all neighbors at the current depth before moving deeper, ensuring the first time you reach the target, it's the shortest way.

possible.name: Dijkstra
possible.desc: The best choice for weighted graphs with non-negative edges. It uses a priority queue to always 'greedy' pick the closest node, guaranteeing an optimal mathematical path through complex networks.

possible.name: Bellman-Ford
possible.desc: A versatile algorithm that can handle weights and negative edges. It works by relaxing all edges repeatedly, making it slower than Dijkstra but much more robust against edge cases like negative cycles.

possible.name: Floyd-Warshall
possible.desc: A powerful Dynamic Programming approach that calculates the shortest distance between EVERY single pair of nodes in the graph simultaneously. Essential for dense graphs.

r.reason: Dijkstra provides the perfect balance of speed and accuracy for most weighted routing problems found in competitive programming.

possible.name: Two Pointers
possible.desc: Uses two markers (left and right) that move toward each other or in the same direction. It's incredibly efficient because it processes the entire array in a single pass without extra memory.

possible.name: Hash Map
possible.desc: Stores every element you see in a 'lookup table' for instant access later. It's like having an index in a book—instead of reading every page, you jump straight to the information you need.

possible.name: Binary Search
possible.desc: Continuously splits a sorted search range in half. It is miraculously fast, able to find one specific value among millions of items in just a few dozen steps.

possible.name: Brute Force
possible.desc: The most straightforward 'check everything' approach. It uses nested loops to compare every element with every other element. Simple to implement but generally only works for small datasets.

r.reason: For general searching or frequency counting, the Hash Map (Dictionary) is your most powerful tool, trading a small amount of memory for incredible O(N) execution speed.

thayeebshaik45@gmail.com.q:
You are given four numbers num1, den1, num2, and den2. You need to find (num1/den1)+(num2/den2) and output the result in the form of (numx/denx).

Input Format:
The first line of input contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases . Then T test cases follow . Each test case contains four integers num1, den1, num2, den2 respectively .

Output Format:
For each test case, in a new line,  output will be the fraction in the form a/b where the fraction denotes the sum of the two given fractions in reduced form.

Your Task:
Since this is a function problem, you don't need to worry about the testcases. Your task is to complete the function addFraction  which adds the two fractions and prints the resulting fraction. The function takes four arguments num1, den1, num2, den2 where num1, num2 denotes the numerators of two fractions and den1, den2 denotes their denominators.

Constraints:
1 <= T <= 100
1 <= den1,den2,num1,num2 <= 1000

Example:
Input
1
1 500 2 500
Output
3/500

possible.name: Two Pointers
possible.desc: Uses two markers (left and right) that move toward each other or in the same direction. It's incredibly efficient because it processes the entire array in a single pass without extra memory.

possible.name: Hash Map
possible.desc: Stores every element you see in a 'lookup table' for instant access later. It's like having an index in a book—instead of reading every page, you jump straight to the information you need.

possible.name: Binary Search
possible.desc: Continuously splits a sorted search range in half. It is miraculously fast, able to find one specific value among millions of items in just a few dozen steps.

possible.name: Brute Force
possible.desc: The most straightforward 'check everything' approach. It uses nested loops to compare every element with every other element. Simple to implement but generally only works for small datasets.

r.reason: For general searching or frequency counting, the Hash Map (Dictionary) is your most powerful tool, trading a small amount of memory for incredible O(N) execution speed.
